data.title: Week Ahead: Fundamentals And Technicals Give Greenlight To Sell Dollars
data.description:
The market was caught leaning the wrong way. Anticipation that Federal Reserve Governor Powell was going to push against speculation of a September rate cut had underpinned the US dollar and short-term rates. The S&P 500 had sold off for five consecutive sessions, the longest losing streak of the year through August 21. Powell said two things that spurred a rally in US financial assets and triggered a sharp and broad dollar sell-off. The Fed Chair said that the "situation suggests downside risks to employment are rising" and that the "shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting policy." Moreover, we note that the early forecasts point to another poor employment report, due September 5. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for nonfarm payrolls to rise by 83k (73k in July) and for unemployment to rise to 4.3% from 4.2%.

As August winds down, and ahead of a long US (and Canadian) holiday weekend, the high frequency economic data does not appear to have the gravitas to substantially impact policy views. The price action itself is the story. The price action ahead of the week confirms the dollar's sensitivity to changes in interest rates, contrary to the conventional wisdom (e.g., here). The US needs to offer investors higher interest rates to support the dollar and policy rates are headed in the opposite direction. The policy divergence between the Fed and most other G10 central banks (leaving aside the BOJ) is likely to widen in a direction that will likely continue to weigh on the dollar. Nor does President Trump's threat to fire Fed Governor Cook if she does not resign does not set well with the market that sees it as another attempt to encroach on the independence of the central bank. In this context, Governor Waller's offer of strong defense of the Fed's independence may diminish his chances of becoming chair, while the likelihood of Powell remaining on the board as governor after his term as chair ends (May 2026) appears to have increased.

US

Drivers: The key to the dollar's rally in July and its heavier tone this month is the shift in US interest rates and Fed expectations. Consider that the 10-year Treasury yield rose nearly 15 bp in July and gave is off a dozen basis points this month. The two-year yield rose almost 24 bp in July and is off around 25 bp here in August. The implied year-end effective Fed funds rate (weighted average) rose 33 bp in July and is also about 22 bp lower this month. Fed Chair Powell acknowledged that the "shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting policy" and warned that "downside risks to employment" may be rising.

Data: There are economic reports nearly every day this week, but they do not appear to have the heft to significantly change the market's conviction that the Federal Reserve will resume unwinding the restrictiveness of the current monetary setting. Fed officials have eschewed survey data. The decline of Boeing orders (303 in May, 116 in June, and 31 in July) set the stage for the second consecutive decline in durable orders and the third in four months. Excluding aircraft and defense orders, core capital goods orders fell by an average of 0.1% in Q2, the first negative quarter in a year. Personal income and consumption remain firm and may have advanced sequentially, though when adjusted for inflation, personal consumption expenditures were flat in H1 after rising 2.4% in H2 24 and 1.2% in H1 24. The data in the CPI and PPI allow economists to have a good handle on the PCE deflator, which the Fed targets. A 0.3% rise in both the headline and core deflators will, depending on the rounding, lift the year rates to 2.7%-2.8% and 2.9%-3.0%, respectively. The question is not whether the tariffs will boost prices but whether the price pressure will be sustained.

Prices: In broad terms, after falling for the first six months of the year, the dollar recovered in July. It gave some portion back in the first part of August and traded firmer last week, helped by the backing up of US interest rates, until Powell's speech at Jackson Hole. The high for the week was recorded before the weekend near 98.85 before reversing sharply and settled below the previous day's range (~98.20) for a bearish outside down day. The break of the 97.80 area may be an early warning of the risk of a return to the July 1 low that was slightly below 96.40.

EMU

Drivers: The euro is supported by the fact that it is a liquid and deep alternative to the US dollar. The two-year interest rate discount for holding euros has diminished. The market has turned more dovish toward the Fed and continues to pare its dovish ECB wagers. The year-end deposit rate, projected by the overnight swaps market is above near 1.85%, the highest since the end of Q1.

Data: Eurozone data is largely limited to the M3 and the associated lending figures, for which the market is not particularly sensitive to, and the ECB's survey of inflation expectations. The one-year expectation was at 2.6% in June and the three-year expectation was at 2.4%. They were at 2.8% and 2.4%, respectively, in July 2024. The eurozone's July CPI was 2.0% higher year-over-year.

Prices: After correcting lower in July (to slightly below $1.14), the euro recovered in the first half of August and reached $1.1730 on August 13. On the back of higher US rates, the dollar recovered broadly and sent the euro through the (38.2%) retracement of the gains in the first part of the month found near $1.1600. The low for the week, recorded before the weekend, was slightly below $1.1585, but Powell's apparent endorsement of a September rate cut sent the euro to a new high for the month (a little above $1.1740). It settled above the previous session high (~$1.1665), forging a bullish outside up day. The trendline connecting the July highs comes in near $1.1740 on Monday. The $1.1800-30 is the next important technical area. Our call for $1.20 before the end of the year may still prove to be conservative.

PRC

Drivers: Officials are determined to keep the yuan broadly stable against the US dollar. It is accepting minor appreciation. Many foreign observers focus on the large trade surplus and argue for a re-valuation of the yuan. As we have noted, the relationship between trade and foreign exchange levels is not simple. Japan, we have noted, records a trade deficit, while by most metrics, the yen is undervalued. Switzerland, which according to the OECD's model of purchasing power parity, is the only G10 currency that is overvalued relative to the dollar, reported trade surplus in H1 25 that was more than a third larger than H1 24, which itself was about 19% greater than the surplus in H1 23. On the other hand, with deflationary forces still gripping the economy, currency appreciation and the implication of tighter financial conditions are not the traditional economic prescription.

Data: The yuan, closely managed, does not appear to be sensitive to high-frequency data. Still, the only data point of note is the July industrial profits. In June, industrial profits fell 4.3% year-over-year, offsetting the 4.1% gain reported in 2024. In June 2023, industrial profits were 8.3% lower year-over-year. This seems to stem from China's over-investment and the competition for market share rather than profits. Beijing recognizes this and has launched a campaign to check it.

Prices: Even as the PBOC has gradually lowered the dollar's reference rate, the greenback has not continued to fall. It bottomed a month ago, slightly below CNY7.15. The greenback fell to a new low for the month before the weekend slightly near CNY7.1635. The US dollar posted its lowest close against the offshore yuan in nearly a month before the weekend (~CNH7.1715).

Japan

Drivers: The yen's exchange rate remains highly sensitive to changes in US interest rates. Consider that over the past 30-day, the correlation of the changes in the exchange rate and the US two-year yield is ~0.80, while the correlation with the two-year JGB is near zero and has fluctuated with year between -0.40 in March to a little more than 0.35 in early June. The correlation between changes in the two-year yield differential and the exchange rate is slightly below the correlation with the US two-year. The correlation with the 10-year yield tells a similar story. The rolling 30-day correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the 10-year Treasury yield is near 0.75, while the correlation with the 10-year JGB is slightly inverted.

Data: The Q2 GDP was stronger than expected at 1.0% at an annualized rate and Q1 GDP was revised to 0.6% from -0.2%. The July industrial production, employment, and retail sales figures are due at the end of the coming week. It will show the kind of momentum at the start of Q3. While retail sales look firm, industrial output may have contracted. However, more important for the Bank of Japan may be the signal from the Tokyo CPI, which offers important insight into the national figure that will be reported in several weeks. All the key measures are expected to have softened. Ahead of the data, the swaps market has almost 20 bp of tightening discounted for this year. The risk of a move next month is seen as minor, which leaves two meetings. The swaps market is pricing about a 50% chance of a quarter-point hike in October and nearly 80% chance of a hike before the end of the year.

Prices: The dollar rose to almost JPY148.80 area at the end of last week. It was the highest level since August 1 and was above the (50%) retracement of this month's losses (~JPY148.55). Yet, Powell's apparent endorsement of September rate cut saw US rates and the dollar tumble. The dollar reversed lower and fell slightly below JPY146.60, which was a new low for the week. The poor settlement was the lowest since July 23. The lower end of the range is around JPY146, and the greenback has not traded below it since July 4. The trendline connecting the April and July lows comes in near JPY144.85 on Monday and almost JPY145.10 at the end of the week.

UK

Drivers: The broad direction of the dollar, reflected in the Dollar Index, is a key factor in sterling's exchange rate. The 30-day correlation of the changes in the two is nearly 0.80. The correlation with changes in the euro is a little less (~0.76). Here too, we see the exchange rate more sensitive (higher correlation) with US rates than UK rates. To wit: the 30-day correlation of changes in the exchange rate and the two-year US yield is almost -0.50, while the correlation with the two-year Gilt is barely positive. The correlation with the two-year rate differential (UK-US) is almost -0.25.

Data: It is a light week for UK macro data. The BRC August shop price index and the CBI retail reports typically do not inspire trading.

Prices: Sterling overshot the (61.8%) retracement of last month's slide, reaching almost $1.3600 on August 14. It subsequently traded lower and reached almost $1.3390 ahead of the weekend before Powell's comments saw it scream higher. Sterling rose to almost $1.3545 to approach the week's high set on Monday slightly above $1.3565. Sterling looks poised to re-test $1.3600. A move above there will set sights on the July 1 high near $1.3790 but there may be potential toward $1.40 in the coming months.

Canada

Drivers: The Canadian dollar remains sensitive to the US dollar's broad direction. The 30-day correlation of changes in the US dollar against the Canadian dollar and the Dollar Index is a little above 0.75. This has not been above 0.80 in more than a year. In early February, the correlation was near an 18-month low around 0.20. The exchange rate's sensitivity to changes in the US S&P 500, we have used as a metric for risk appetites, has slackened. The 30-day correlation was near -0.50 at the start of the year and was almost +0.45 in early June and is slightly positive now. The 100-day rolling correlation is -0.15, which is the least inverse correlation since early 2018.

Data: The monthly GDP shows the Canadian economy contracted by 0.1% in April and May, after growing by a monthly average of 0.13% in Q1. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipates 0.1% expansion in June, while projecting a 0.3%-0.5% annualized contraction in Q2 (two different surveys) after 2.2% growth in Q1 25. The economy is seen doing slightly better than stagnating in Q3.

Prices: The Canadian dollar does not fit the general pattern we have seen in the other currencies. It weakened to three-month lows, and even the stronger than expected retail sales (ex-autos, up 1.9%, after three consecutive monthly declines) were unable to stem the rot. The US dollar's low for the year was set in mid-June near CAD1.3540. Higher lows were set in early and late July. The greenback took out the August 1 high (~CAD1.3880) last week and rose through CAD1.3900, reaching CAD1.3925 before the weekend. The Canadian dollar showed little reaction to the stronger than expected retail sales (1.9% excluding autos, well above expectations and more than offset the outright decline of the previous three months). But then Powell spoke, and the greenback was sold to a three-day low near CAD1.3810. As often is the case in a weak US dollar environment, the Canadian dollar underperformed. Its 0.70% gain against the dollar was the least among the G10 currencies.

Australia

Drivers: The correlation between changes in the Australian dollar and Dollar Index over the past 30 sessions is a little more than -0.80, the most extreme in a year. The 100-day correlation is near -0.60, which is around the middle this year's range. Over the past 30 sessions, the correlation with the changes in the Canadian dollar is about -0.80, near the most extreme since March.

Data: There are two highlights this week. The first is the record from this month's central bank meeting that resulted in a quarter-point rate cut. The impact may be most acute on the expectations for next month's meeting. The futures market has about a 1-in-3 chance of a cut on September 30, but another cut fully discounted by the end of the year. The market sees the cash target slightly below 3.0% (from 3.6% currently), which is currently projected to be the terminal rate.

Prices: The Australian dollar, like the Canadian dollar, fell to a new low for the month last week near $0.6415. It recovered smartly in response to Powell's comments and briefly poked above $0.6500. In one fell swoop, it retraced more than half of its losses since this month's high set August 14 (~$0.6570) and approached the (61.8%) retracement near $0.6510. The (50%) retracement of the decline since the year's high was recorded (July 24 ~$0.6625) is near $0.6520, with the (61.8%) retracement close to $0.6545.

Mexico

Drivers: The broad decline in the dollar has lifted emerging market currencies. The JP Morgan Emerging Market Currency Index is up about 6.75% so far this year, while the MSCI version has risen by around 6.0% this year. The Mexican peso has appreciated by around 11.3%. This puts it in seventh place among the best performing emerging market currencies this year. Central European currencies, including the Russian ruble, account for five of the best and the Brazilian real's nearly 13% gain this year puts it in the top four.

Data: Mexico reports July's trade balance. In the first six months of the year, Mexico recorded a $1.4 bln surplus. In H1 24, Mexico had a trade deficit of $10.9 bln and a $6.98 bln deficit in H1 23. The last time that Mexico had a surplus in the first six months of the year was in 2021 (~$1.37 bln). Exports have risen by almost 4.4% and imports have risen by nearly 1.8%. July unemployment is also due. It averaged 2.66% in Q2, the highest quarterly average since Q3 24. It stood at almost 2.70% in June, up from 2.43% at the end of 2024 but down slightly from last June's 2.78% unemployment rate. The central bank's inflation report is also due. New economic forecasts will be presented. The current target rate is 7.75% and the swaps market has two more quarter point cuts discounted. The risk is on the downside, assuming the US growth slows, and the Fed's rate cuts resume.

Prices: The dollar had been coiling in a narrow range set on Monday (~MXN18.7120-MXN18.8675) and broke down before the weekend to nearly MXN18.57. The MXN18.50 area must go to be anything of technical significance. The broad sideways movement puts the momentum traders and trend followers as a disadvantage. The carry for dollar-based investors is important. Consider that so far here in Q3 the peso has risen by about 0.85%, the third best among emerging market currencies, but with the carry (interest rate pick up), the peso has returned a little more than 2% to dollar-based investors. Among emerging market currencies, Latam currencies count for three of four of the top six total returns among here in Q3 (Colombian peso ~3.55%, Turkish lira ~3.0%, South African rand ~2.60%, Brazilian real ~2.3%, Mexican peso ~2.05%, and the Peruvian sol ~1.60%).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wall Street Week Ahead
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Wall Street's attention this week will be squarely on Nvidia (NVDA), as the chipmaker gears up for its latest quarterly results. A key inflation gauge and another reading on economic growth will also grab some of the spotlight.

Nvidia (NVDA), the world's largest publicly listed company by market cap, will announce fiscal second quarter results on Wednesday. Its reports have become market-moving events, and its blockbuster quarterly performances have spoiled investors. With the artificial intelligence trade back in play, Nvidia's (NVDA) commentary on its AI chips and on China will be closely watched.

On the economic calendar, Thursday will see the first revision for second quarter gross domestic product (GDP) growth. And then on Friday, market participants will receive the July reading for the core personal consumption expenditures price index - widely seen as the Federal Reserve's preferred inflation gauge.

Investing Group Spotlight

These days, it seems everyone, from Wall Street to Main Street, is singing the praises of artificial intelligence-themed investment opportunities. Andres Cardenal has been investing in AI from its infancy, and staying ahead of the technology curve seems to be built into his DNA. Through his Seeking Alpha Investing Group, The Data Driven Investor, Andres has beaten key tech benchmarks like Cathie Woods' ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) by a cumulative 78% going back to 2018. This is achieved via high-conviction, well-researched long-term growth ideas and disciplined risk management picked up during Andres' 25 years working with hedge funds, family offices, and asset managers across the Americas.

Two of Andres' current top ideas:

Alibaba (BABA) (Free write-up)
BABA’s AI products have delivered triple-digit Y/Y growth for seven consecutive quarters, the cloud business is accelerating, and the company is boosting performance in commerce and advertising by infusing AI into these segments. Nevertheless, investors seem to be missing the forest for the trees, with the stock trading at just 13.8x forward earnings estimates.

Micron (MU) (Free write-up)
While most of the focus in AI investing has been on large language models and the software side of the business, the most potent phase of the AI revolution will be robotics, as this means AI getting involved in the physical world. As robots require strong memory for tasks like object recognition and autonomous decision-making, MU is perfectly positioned to benefit. At just 9.6x forward earnings estimates, MU is simply too cheap to ignore.

These two ideas really only scratch the surface. Andres is currently offering a 20% discount for first-time subscribers. Learn More >>

data.title: Asana: Fading Growth With No Medium Term Catalyst
data.description:
To say that Asana (ASAN) has been a disappointing stock over the past couple of years would be an understatement — the company's valuation dropped by 36% since my first article on the company two years ago and by 44% since February this year alone.

While the dip might seem like a compelling entry point for some investors, I still don't see any reason to own the stock beyond pure speculation. Asana's growth has not stopped slowing, there are no short- or medium-term catalysts on the horizon, and AI might actually cause more trouble for the company. Therefore, I maintain my hold rating and recommend avoiding the stock.

Asana's growth is fading fast

The biggest negative point about Asana is its revenue growth rates, which have been slowing down fast in the last 3-4 years. In the latest quarter, the company's revenue increased by only 8.6% year-over-year, compared to 13.2% in FQ1 2025 and 57.4% in FQ1 2023. While positive growth is better than nothing, Asana's current sales expansion rates are dismally low for a software company, especially when even such $3+ trillion hardware-focused companies like Apple (AAPL) can grow at a faster rate.

If a comparison with Apple is not convincing, Asana has also been growing significantly slower than any of its work collaboration software and SaaS peers. Most recently, monday.com (MNDY) reported its Q2 results, demonstrating 26.6% year-over-year revenue growth. Even Atlassian (TEAM), with its $6 billion in annual revenue and $44 billion valuation, grew its sales by 23% in the latest quarter. This shows the magnitude of Asana's growth problem and already makes the stock less compelling than other software companies in the market.

Importantly, Asana's sales have been decreasing despite the fact that the company has been spending substantial amounts of money on sales and marketing. In the last three full fiscal years (2023-2025), Asana had $1.25 billion in S&M expenses, with only about $963 million in research and development and $1.92 billion in total revenue. To compare, Asana's direct competitor monday.com spent $1.36 billion on S&M in the last three full years, with $2.22 billion in total revenue and more than 30% revenue growth in the last two years, compared to 10-20% for Asana. This should demonstrate how inefficient Asana's investments have been so far.

AI is not likely to save the company in the short term

This growth dynamic is not expected to change any time soon. The company itself projects a 7-9% revenue growth in its fiscal 2026, (Asana's fiscal year ends in January), which is even lower than the 11% in fiscal 2025. Beyond that, consensus revenue estimates for Asana also remain low, with an 8.7% revenue growth projected for fiscal 2027 and a 10.6% growth for fiscal 2028.

It is possible that some growth acceleration might happen if Asana's AI products find any success — the company is betting big on its AI Studio as a future growth driver. In FQ1, the company reported that AI Studio achieved more than $1 million in annual recurring revenue; Asana also introduced additional tiers for its AI offerings to expand adoption. Even though I personally remain skeptical about the value Asana's AI products might bring, there could be some revenue potential here if customers actually continue enjoying the product beyond early adoption.

However, the generally increasing popularity of AI might also influence the company negatively in the future and make Asana's growth even more difficult. The issue is that Asana might face a similar problem to what monday.com shared during its latest earnings call — the acquisition funnel has become more tricky as Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) pushes AI summaries as top search results. This commentary was partly the reason behind the 32% stock price drop in MNDY.

As Roy said, we are seeing some softness within the down market due to the changes in the Google algorithm.

Now, Asana might be impacted even more by these dynamics. In the latest quarter, the company demonstrated a 95% net dollar retention rate; a figure below 100% means the company's revenue from existing customers goes down over time. This puts much higher pressure on acquisition, which, again, is getting more difficult due to the changes in the Google algorithm. monday.com might not be affected as much since its total dollar retention rate is a solid 111%, and the figure goes as high as 117% for high-paying customers.

As a result, while Asana's AI products might potentially spark some interest over time, the company might suffer from the general AI trends in search in the medium term. This is not to mention that its direct competitor monday.com has also been expanding its AI offerings much faster, and I would expect more customers to choose monday.com over Asana at the moment.

Asana's valuation is not compelling enough in the current market

Even though ASAN's price has dropped more than 90% from its 2022 highs, the stock's valuation remains too high for what it offers, especially after the recent market events. Asana now trades with the highest P/E ratio among its peers, and, after the post-earnings drop in MNDY, ASAN is now even more expensive than its competitor.

Meanwhile, Asana's fundamentals are less appealing than its competitors like monday.com or Atlassian. As I mentioned above, its revenue growth has been much slower than that of its peers, and ASAN's profitability is nowhere near the numbers of MNDY, TEAM, or other work software stocks.

The market does estimate significant earnings increases for Asana in the next two fiscal years, with a 48% growth estimated for fiscal 2027 and a 38% growth for fiscal 2028. However, I personally don't believe in these numbers because a) the revenue growth for the same period is estimated to be around just 8-10%, b) it might become more difficult and expensive for Asana to attract new customers with the increasing popularity of AI search, and c) the peers offer better and more comprehensive products at the moment, so Asana will need to increase its R&D spend even more to remain competitive.

Additionally, all three Seeking Alpha rating components give ASAN a Hold rating and a mediocre score of around 3. Seeking Alpha Quant also gives pessimistic D grades for ASAN's valuation, profitability, and momentum.

Upcoming earnings and what could change the thesis

Asana is scheduled to report its FQ2 earnings on September 3, and while I don't expect any positive surprises, here are the things to watch for and what could change my mind about the stock:

  • Net dollar retention rate (NDRR): this is the most important component of the report for me. If Asana finally manages to turn the trend around and demonstrate a growing NDRR, the company might become an interesting turnaround play.
  • Sales and marketing costs in combination with new customer additions: if Asana is able to grow its new customers without significant increases in S&M, avoiding the issues I mentioned above, it could become a catalyst for better profitability in the future.
  • AI product adoption: Asana might be able to turn around if the company's AI offerings start generating real money. The corporation mentioned $1 million in ARR from AI Studio in FQ1, which is negligible. If this figure expands significantly, it will be a positive sign for me.

Key takeaways

Overall, despite a 92% drop in Asana's stock price from its highs in 2022, there is still no light at the end of the tunnel. With revenue growth slowing down significantly to just 7-9%, a dollar retention rate of just 95%, and valuation still too high for a seemingly declining company, ASAN is best avoided at the moment. While Asana's AI offerings might spark some interest among the company's customers in the future, there is still no evidence for that. Meanwhile, it's best to stay on the sidelines.

data.title: JBS N.V.: High Debt And High Dividend Dilemma
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I grant JBS N.V. (JBS) a hold rating. My main argument is that JBS has significant debt levels. In Q2 2025, the company had a 77.89% liabilities-to-assets ratio. Consequently, more than three-fourths of the company is in debt. I believe this value represents a deterrent to considering JBS a safe investment opportunity. In addition, JBS has been involved in a series of controversies, such as bribing Brazilian authorities and manipulating prices.

With the previous paragraph, I am not saying that the company will have a negative outlook. I think the company could expand its operations in the following years. In fact, JBS is the company with the highest growth outlook among its peers. It pays a 14.97% dividend yield, an attractive value. Furthermore, the company is the largest global poultry and beef producer. As such, it benefits from economies of scale.

Corn and soybean prices have experienced a significant decline. These commodities represent a significant component of JBS's cost of sales. Therefore, JBS is expected to increase its profit margin. However, when pondering all the positive and negative aspects of JBS, I still believe it is time to wait.

The Company

JBS is a Brazilian company headquartered in São Paulo, Brazil. It was founded in 1953 by Jose Batista Sobrinho, who began a small slaughterhouse. In 1953, the slaughterhouse had a capacity of approximately 500 head of cattle per day, increasing to 5,800 head of cattle per day in 1998. By 2007, the company debuted on the Brazilian stock market. That year, it also expanded its operations in the U.S. As of 2024, it had operations in North America, Latin America, Europe, the Middle East, Asia, and Oceania. As such, its global presence is one of its characteristics. According to JBS, it is the largest global poultry and beef producer. Additionally, the company indicates that it ranks second as a pork producer globally.

Segments

In the first half of 2025, the Beef North America segment accounted for 32% of the company's revenues, as depicted in the following chart. In this segment, the company has nine beef processing facilities in the U.S. and one in Canada. JBS buys cattle and processes them in its facilities, then the company generates its revenues by selling fresh and value-added beef. Moving to the Pilgrim's Pride Segment, which accounts for 22% of the company's revenues, it processes and sells chickens in different presentations. In this segment, the company has vertical integration. Consequently, JBS does not have to buy broilers from resellers.

On the other hand, Brazil and Seara segments have vertical integration in the cattle and poultry industry, respectively. Both operations are located in Brazil, which accounts for 26% of the company's revenues.

In the first half of 2025, the Seara and Pilgrim's Pride segments were the most profitable, as depicted in the following chart. Both segments sell chicken products. Meanwhile, the Brazil and Beef North American segments are among the less profitable ones. These segments commercialized beef in different presentations.

Debt

I think debt is the primary risk factor of JBS. The first chart below shows that the company reported a 77.89% liabilities-to-assets ratio in Q2 2025. This ratio declined in the last quarter, but it is still a high value. It indicates that more than three-fourths of the company is in debt. In June of this year, JBS issued $3.5 million in bonds, adding more fuel to its liabilities.

Due to the significant debt levels, the company will have to face significant expenses to repay its debt in the upcoming years, as depicted in the second chart above. For instance, in 2027, it will have to pay $1,013 million. Meanwhile, in 2032, it will have to pay $2,911 million.

The critical point is whether JBS has substantial resources to meet its obligations. The following chart illustrates that the operating expenses represent a significant portion of the company's operating profits. To provide context, in 2024, it reported an operating profit of $4,378 million and finance expenses of $2,389 million. As such, I think that if the prices of pork, chicken, and beef encounter an unfavorable scenario, the company could experience financial distress, which could lead the company to sell some of its core operations.

For illustration purposes, I will provide the following example: Imagine that the company reduces its long-term debt from $19,130 million to 9,130 million, for a $10,000 million decline. If this occurs, the company's liabilities-to-assets ratio will drop to 53.97%, assuming all else is constant. At this level, more than half of the company will still be composed of debt. However, it is a lower debt level than the current debt that the company has.

Between 2022 and 2024, JBS invested $5,183 million in CapEx. As such, if the company reduces its long-term debt by $10,000 million, it will have to sacrifice its investment in capital expenditures and pay fewer dividends for several years. Consequently, the company will see a reduction in its capacity to create value for its investors.

This section will be incomplete without including other companies in the analysis. The following chart shows the debt-to-equity and debt-to-free cash flow ratios of JBS, Tyson Foods (TSN), BRF S.A. (BRFS), Hormel Foods (HRL), Pilgrim's Pride (PPC), Smithfield Foods (SFD), and Seaboard Corporation (SEB). These companies are relevant producers of chicken, beef, and pork. JBS is the company with the highest debt-to-equity ratio by a large margin. In addition, it has a higher debt-to-free cash flow ratio than the median in the peer group. Therefore, JBS shows higher debt levels than its peers.

Controversies

JBS has been involved in series of controversies. I think these aspects are important to mention, since they may occur again. Approximately, between 2005 and 2014, JBS paid bribes to Brazilian functionaries of the development bank BNDES. For these payments, JBS received financial funding from the previous institution.

In 2020, the SEC and the Department of Justice fined JBS for more than $280 million. This fine was for the bribery scheme conducted by JBS to acquire Pilgrim's Pride. In addition, this year, JBS paid $83.5 million in antitrust claims in order to curb the meat supply and inflate prices in the beef market artificially.

I think that the negative aspects discussed in this section undermine JBS's reputation. Investors could think twice before investing in JBS. It is safer to invest in other companies that do not present this negative track record.

Agricultural Commodities

Corn and soybean prices have declined, as depicted in the following chart. The prices of these agricultural commodities have reached their level observed during 2014-2020, a period of low prices. As such, JBS will benefit from the decline in the corn and soybean prices. I worked in the agribusiness industry. I learned that corn and soybeans are the primary expenses that farmers incur to raise hogs and broilers. I link the following articles for readers who want to see the proportion of corn and soybeans that are used in hogs' and broilers' animal feed.

In 2024, approximately 44% of JBS's sales were due to its chicken and pork sales (Pork U.S., and Pilgrim's Pride segments). Due to the decline in corn and soybean prices, JBS will experience higher profit margins. The rating firm, Fitch, indicates that JBS will improve its operational margin due to lower grain prices, which is in line with the analysis presented in this section.

Positive Aspects

So far, I have discussed mostly negative aspects of JBS. In this section, I will discuss some positive aspects that could increase the company's revenues and earnings in the upcoming years. Consequently, these factors could unlock value for investors.

Recently, JBS was listed on the New York Stock Exchange. Since its inception, the company's stock price has increased by 18.29%. Therefore, the market has reacted positively. With this move, JBS will have access to funds in the U.S. For instance, the company could issue common shares to reduce its debt levels or finance its expansion plan.

Another positive aspect is JBS's growth outlook. The following table shows some growth ratios of JBS, TSN, BRFS, HRL, PPC, SFD, and SEB. For 2025, the analyst consensus expects a 6.52% revenue growth for JBS, which is the highest value among its peers. In addition, over a five-year period, JBS achieved a 15.33% CAGR in its revenues, the highest value. Furthermore, the analyst consensus projects a 22.20% EBITDA CAGR in 2025. As such, the growth perspective suggests a positive outlook for the company.

The profitability ratios indicate that JBS exhibits higher profit margins compared to its peers. JBS has a 15.03% gross profit margin, while the median of this ratio is 13.79%, as depicted in the chart below. In addition, it achieves a 25.44% return on equity, considerably higher than those reported by its peers.

Fitch grants a BBB- rating to JBS. This value indicates that it is a solid company and that the probability that it will default on one of its bonds is low. Fitch said that JBS is a profitable firm. It considers that the company will achieve a 2.2 debt-to-EBITDA ratio in 2025 and a ratio of 1.8 for 2026. With these values, Fitch affirmed that JBS will have resources to invest in its operations and pay dividends.

Dividends are another positive aspect. Currently, JBS pays a 14.97% dividend yield, which is the highest value among its peers, as depicted in the following chart. At that level, an investor will recuperate their investment in less than 7 years. To provide context, the ICE BofA CCC & Lower US High Yield Index has an effective yield of 12.09%. JBS pays a higher yield, despite having a BBB- rating. This difference could indicate that JBS represents an interesting investment opportunity.

The other positive advantage is the scale of JBS's operations. The company is the largest chicken and beef producer in the world. In addition, it is the second-largest global producer of pork. As such, JBS benefits from economies of scale. Furthermore, its primary operations are located in the U.S. and Brazil. The U.S. is the largest corn producer, and Brazil is the largest soybean producer. Consequently, it can buy corn and soybeans in these countries and export them for its operations in Europe, Asia, and Oceania at a lower price, since it has a network in the U.S. and Brazil.

Moving to the valuation ratios, they suggest that JBS is undervalued compared to its peers. For instance, the P/E of JBS is 7.14, while the median ratio is 10.97. In addition, the EV/EBITDA also indicates that JBS presents an undervaluation. I believe that the undervaluation is due to the high debt levels of JBS.

Overall, I think the company presents an excellent growth outlook. In addition, it is a profitable company. Furthermore, it presents a high dividend yield. Furthermore, corn and soybean prices have declined. As such, if the prices of these raw materials remain at low levels, JBS will increase its profit margin. However, the company has significant debt levels.

Population

JBS has a global presence with operations on all continents. As such, the population growth could boost the company's operations. In 2022, the global population was 8 billion, and it is expected to reach 9.7 billion by 2050, representing a 21.25% potential increase. In addition, the global gross domestic product [GDP] per capita increased by 148.76% from 2000 to 2024. It reached $13,673 per capita in 2024. If this figure continues to increase, it will increase the demand for meat worldwide, potentially increasing JBS's revenues.

Long-Term Strategy

Acquisitions and a global presence have contributed to JBS's expansion. The company has a global presence, which contributes to an easier expansion into new markets. For instance, JBS can acquire new companies in Brazil, since it has a presence in that country. In fact, it acquired a 50% stake in the company Mantequeira, a Brazilian firm, in January of this year. This company is the largest egg producer in South America, producing 4 billion eggs annually. This acquisition will likely have a positive impact on the company in the future.

Furthermore, JBS expects to introduce new products with more value-added. In 2024, the company had one segment, in which most of its revenues came from value-added products, as depicted in the following table. This segment is Seara. On the other hand, the revenues from the other segments mainly came from fresh or frozen products, without added value. If the company has been successful in introducing new products with these characteristics, its revenues and profit margin will be positively impacted. To add context, Seara is the segment with the highest EBITDA margin.

Conclusion

In light of the analysis presented in this article, I think that JBS has significant debt levels that could undermine its growth potential. In Q2 2025, it had a 77.89% liabilities-to-assets ratio. I believe that this value is too high to consider JBS to be an excellent investment opportunity. In other words, more than three-fourths of JBS is composed of debt. Furthermore, the company has a history of bribes. Due to this history, governments and institutions may not approve potential acquisitions by JBS.

However, JBS expects an excellent growth outlook. In addition, it pays a dividend yield of 14.97%. The corn and soybean prices have declined, which is a positive aspect for JBS, as it will strengthen the company's profit margin. Finally, the valuation ratios suggest that JBS is undervalued. The elements discussed in this paragraph could indicate that the company will improve its financial position. However, I think that the debt level of JBS is too high for it to be considered an excellent investment opportunity. Consequently, I rate JBS with a hold rating.

data.title: Guidewire: Patient Capital Rewarded, But Entry Requires Discipline
data.description:
Guidewire Software (GWRE) presents a decent buying opportunity for patient capital on improving margin trends and a steady growth story. Valuations are not cheap, however, and do account for current improvements in margin trends. The specialized focus on insurance software solutions with a cloud-first model with recurring subscription revenue has scope for further improvements over the next few quarters. There is a good chance for share price appreciation for growth-minded investors willing to assign a risk premium to tech plays with reasonable performance potential. I believe a close to 15% correction from recent highs makes for a good entry point for those investors. Investors looking for more world-class software hurdles to be met, like the rule of 40 or uncomfortable riding high valuations, may give Guidewire a pass. I belong to the latter group of investors who believe in the fundamentals, but will keep Guidewire as a hold because of valuations.

Opportunity and Edge

Guidewire's AI-enabled P&C insurance solution has stood the test of market validation. This is not a share that needs a proof of concept for the product market fit. Revenues have grown steadily over several years now, and customer stickiness has kicked in. Importantly, with topline maturity, Guidewire has not necessarily seen slacking growth. In fact, since 2024, growth has rebounded to an extent. Even in Q3 FY2025, Guidewire reported a steady 22% growth. Support and subscription revenue was up 32% YoY.

Competitive advantages and moats are well established now. In specialized solutions, expansion is often a critical moat that enables future upsells on product enhancements, even if market saturation signals show up. In the case of Guidewire, saturation does not seem to be a problem. The strong customer base takes topline concerns off the table for this thesis.

Margins and Profitability

A significant EPS beat in Q3 2025 signaled improved profitability, and stocks soared after the results. That has led to upward revisions of expectations for Q4, with results due in a few days. The profitability trends are encouraging but still not at world-class levels. Gross margins are still below SaaS benchmarks. The TTM gross margin of 62% is on an upward trajectory, though showing greater efficiencies through scale. Most of the growth in the past year is attributable to growth in subscriptions and support. There the margins are reasonable at 68%. This is a visible improvement from around 63% in subscriptions and support Q3 FY2024.

Even free cash flow, once a concern, has improved in the past few quarters. The current levels of ~20% FCF margins and a 20%+ growth rate tilt GWRE over the 40 benchmark in the Rule for 40. Sustained improvements will enhance the combined growth and margin profile well beyond 40, and that is where I believe the core of any growth investor's buy thesis will lie.

Free cash flow and overall profitability are getting boosted by improving efficiency in SG&A, showing cost efficiencies and better customer acquisition costs. R&D costs are also seeing steady efficiencies of scale. Overall, from a margin perspective, improvements are already underway, and the trend points to further improvements as cloud-based subscription revenues grow further from here on. So far, growth in the subscription segment has been great as well, showing expectations of continued efficiencies are well grounded in executional proof.

Valuations are Deal Breaking

While the fundamentals look well aligned for continued growth and improving margins, valuations point to a more cautious story. The forward EV to revenue ratio of ~13.25x, is slightly down from recent highs of ~17x, but remains at the upper end of the vertical SaaS spectrum in my view. For ~13x EV to forward revenues, I will be looking for higher gross margins and free cash flow conversion levels. The valuations are also relatively elevated compared to Guidewire's own recent historical averages.

It appears like most of the continued moderate growth at scale and a shift to a recurring revenue model boosting margins are roughly factored in at this point.

A Strong Hold, But Wait for Fresh Buys

Guidewire is a case of a strong hold for me bordering on Buy. And valuations are clearly the deterrent. I will look for at least a drop to ~10x in price to forward revenue ratios to get in. And that is the only watchlist item for me, because I am relatively confident of the margin improvement path. Execution risks are not what stops me from making fresh buys. That said, the 15% correction and drop in valuations in recent times almost makes for an opportunity for investors with higher risk tolerance.

With a quarterly earnings session round the corner, I expect revenue estimates to be met in Q4 FY2025. Markets and analyst estimates have already upgraded revenue estimates for the upcoming quarter based on upgraded management guidance. I expect Guidewire to meet the $336 million revenue estimates at the midpoint of the guidance. EPS numbers could beat expectations by a bit, but not like the surprise we saw in Q3. As an interested investor, margin growth and sustained growth in recurring revenues will be of interest, but I will await any significant knee-jerk reactions dragging valuations towards my preferred entry zone.

It is very clear that there is a lot to like about Guidewire otherwise. The cloud migration momentum is definitely great. Guidewire is also positioning itself as a technology innovator in the P&C insurance domain with AI agentic solutions deployment to enhance underwriting and claims operations. Despite its size, it is still showing healthy growth, potentially from geographical expansion in EMEA, APAC, and North America. Expansion into mid-tier insurer opportunities is also underway. Since valuations are already reflecting the strengths, the Hold rating only changes on valuation contractions to Buy. As of now, existing investors can hold on to their positions given the strong fundamentals, but this is patient capital that may need time to grow from here on.

data.title: Nvidia Q2 Preview: AI Bubble Is Popping
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

Heading into Q2 earnings, I continue to be a strong sell on shares of GPU giant Nvidia (NVDA). Despite shares of the major technology company being up roughly 37% over the last six months and nearly 32.54% year-to-date, Nvidia has an unsustainable valuation, slowing growth momentum, and significant geopolitical risks (even with their recent China export license).

I believe this is the beginning of the AI hype cycle cooling, and early signs suggest that the “bubble” in AI infrastructure may be deflating. Nvidia’s outsized gains from GPU sales for large model training could wane, with demand potentially shifting toward cost-efficient inference hardware and smaller models. I believe that in a post-hype environment, Nvidia may not experience the same level of dominance if pre-training capex peaks and alternative solutions take hold.

It’s my belief that even with China export restrictions easing, China sales have a major overhang. The U.S. now-reversed ban on Nvidia’s high-end H20 chips to China has already erased billions of dollars in potential sales, and it has also forced inventory write-offs. At the same time, Chinese players like Huawei are rapidly advancing domestic AI chips, which is increasing local competition. This makes it incredibly clear that Nvidia’s growth is being threatened (even with the export licenses resuming).

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

The main reason I am doing follow-up coverage going into the quarter is because of what I wrote about in my last piece of coverage on Nvidia. Last quarter, I wrote about how the company’s extreme valuation and its exposure in China were major red flags. Now, those concerns are materializing.

Q1 results showed hyper-growth slowing, with revenue up 69% year-over-year, but up only 12% quarter-over-quarter. Since then, Nvidia has taken a $4.5 billion inventory charge tied to unsold China-bound chips, which emphasizes the geopolitical risk warning that I mentioned in my last article.

The company’s once breakneck revenue expansion appears to have plateaued sequentially. Management’s own guidance for FQ2 (~$45 billion in revenue) implies, basically, flat quarter-over-quarter sales, which would be a sharp deceleration from the double-digit quarter-over-quarter gains of recent quarters. I think this suggests that the initial wave of AI infrastructure build-out is maturing, and the easy growth may be behind us.

We’re also starting to see emerging limits in large language model (LLM) pre-training capabilities. The industry is starting to realize that simply scaling models with more GPUs yields diminishing returns. On the enterprise side, I believe customers are now shifting their focus to inference efficiency, as they try to get more out of existing models and hardware. Infrastructure spending is already unsustainable. Nvidia could lose out.

GPT-5 Was A Flop

I believe OpenAI’s GPT-5 launch fell well short of the hype, which I think has dampened urgency for new GPU investments. GPT-5, which some expected to deliver a ‘quantum leap’, delivered only incremental improvements over GPT-4o. Many early users claimed that GPT-5 felt only like an evolution and not a revolution - hardly justifying a massive surge in new training hardware.

Not only were users unimpressed with GPT-5, but it actually sparked backlash. Many users found its answers colder and less impressive than GPT-4o, with shorter, blander answers, according to Medium. The complaints did enough to force OpenAI to reinstate the older GPT-4o model options for paying users within a week. CEO Sam Altman admitted they “totally screwed up” by forcing the switch.

GPT-5 still makes mistakes and falls short of AGI expectations, which reinforces warnings that simply adding more parameters is yielding less bang for the buck. With the wow factor fading, hyperscalers could be rethinking orders for new GPUs. If next-gen models aren’t dramatically more capable, the rush to deploy tens of thousands of additional GPUs for training cools off.

China Chips

The U.S. export ban on Nvidia’s H20 GPUs to China has had a major impact on Nvidia’s business. In April, the government required that H20 chip sales to China obtain a license, which effectively cut off Nvidia’s ability to ship its top AI GPUs freely. The company responded by halting production of the chips, and they instructed suppliers like Amkor and Samsung to suspend work on them.

According to Yahoo Finance:

Nvidia (NVDA) has told some component suppliers to suspend production of its H20 AI chip...Nvidia instructed Arizona-based Amkor Technology to stop production of the H20 chips this week and also notified South Korea's Samsung Electronics.

The company reportedly took a $4.5 billion hit when the rules tightened. In my opinion, this reflected missed sales and inventory write-downs for unsellable high-end chips. This is clearly a huge lost opportunity - revenue that might have been if not for geopolitics.

Nvidia is now facing a real threat from China’s pivot to homegrown silicon. Chinese authorities have actively urged local firms to avoid Nvidia’s H20 chips, citing security concerns and directing them to domestic alternatives. This opens the door for other competitors like Huawei, which in 2025 began mass shipments of its Ascend 910C AI GPU - a chip that combines two previous-gen 910Bs and achieves performance on par with Nvidia’s H100 by design.

In short, local competition is rapidly accelerating. Big players are soon to become the “hardware of choice” for China’s AI developers, and along with startups, Chinese chipmakers are racing to fill the void left by Nvidia. Even though most U.S. restrictions no longer persist, the damage has been done.

Earnings Preview

Analyst consensus for FQ2 2026 (quarter ended July 2025) is coming in at about $46 billion in top line revenue, which would equate to about a 53% year-over-year jump. The FQ2 EPS estimate is currently at $1.01. Investors should be concerned, though. On a sequential basis, this implies virtually flat revenue vs. Q1. This is a huge difference compared to the strong quarterly top line growth that Nvidia experienced last year.

Nvidia’s own guidance for Q2 was ~$45 billion, which essentially confirmed plateauing sales quarter-over-quarter. Even if Nvidia is able to achieve the consensus of $46 billion, it would still only represent a hair of growth sequentially, small after a 12% quarter-over-quarter rise in Q1.

Analysts are also becoming increasingly bearish on Nvidia. In the last three months, there have been 18 downward EPS revisions for the company, with only 16 upward. On the other hand, out of 34 total revenue revisions in the last three months, 21 of them have been downward, with only 13 being upward. To me, this indicates that Wall Street has modestly reined in expectations for Q2 and beyond.

Looking further ahead, the street forecasts continued growth, but at a decelerating rate. For example, FQ3 2026 revenue is estimated around $52.5 billion with $1.19 EPS.

Valuation

With no GPT-5 catalyst and homegrown threats from China, valuation continues to be a major concern for me. Nvidia’s stock trades at ~40x forward earnings (40.38, to be exact), which is a 75.53% premium to the sector median, which is just 23.01.

On a trailing basis, it’s even richer. Nvidia has a TTM Non-GAAP P/E ratio of 54.98, which is nearly 150% higher than the sector median.

On other metrics, the story remains largely unchanged. Nvidia’s TTM price-to-sales ratio is 28.95, which is more than 800% higher than the sector median of 3.16. Because of these numbers, it’s no surprise that Seeking Alpha Quant gives Nvidia an overall valuation grade of an F, with the stock ranking significantly overpriced on virtually every metric.

In my opinion, these lofty valuations hinge on the idea that Nvidia must sustain extraordinary growth. However, we are already starting to see growth normalize. The stock’s premium does not yet fully reflect emerging risks, which I think is dangerous.

I continue to think a re-rating will be inevitable. If Nvidia were to trade at a market-multiple P/E (like 23x forward earnings, in line with peers), the stock would be ~45% lower than today’s price. This isn’t to suggest that there will be an immediate crash, but I definitely don’t see how shares move higher from here.

Bull Thesis

Although I think AI is shifting away from Nvidia, demand for AI GPUs (for now) remains strong, and, for now, Nvidia is still the leader. Even ex-China global hyperscalers and enterprises are pouring resources into AI (sovereign AI) - racing to procure GPUs as they build out large language models. Nvidia’s order books reflect this, with certain customers still buying as many H100s as they can get.

I also believe that Nvidia benefits from an entrenched ecosystem and technological moat. Its proprietary CUDA software platform, with millions of developers and optimized libraries, acts as a powerful lock-in. Competing GPUs often struggle to gain adoption because so much AI software is written for CUDA - a fact that continues to funnel business to Nvidia. On this front, I do believe that competitor AMD (AMD) will break into this market. I wrote about this last year.

However, even with these sovereign AI opportunities, Nvidia still has an uphill battle. By one metric, big tech is set to spend $364 billion on AI investments this year. That’s not sustainable.

Takeaway

Nvidia currently sits at the center of the AI revolution. However, a great company does not always mean it’s a great stock to invest in, and I think this is one of those instances. I continue to reiterate a cautious outlook. While long-term AI demand should keep Nvidia’s business fundamentally strong, the near-term expectations and valuation have run too far ahead of reality.

I continue to worry about a growth slowdown, the overextended valuation, and geopolitical/regulatory hurdles. I think the nature of AI investment is evolving, as GPT-5 showed that AI growth is slowing. There’s a shift from “land grab” spending on massive training clusters to a more measured approach focused on efficiency and inference.

The bottom line is that I maintain my thesis that Nvidia’s stock is priced for perfection in an environment that is becoming less than perfect. With this, I continue to believe that shares of the company are a strong sell.

data.title: John Hancock Disciplined Value International Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review and outlook

International stocks performed very well in the quarter, building on their gain in the first three months of the year. After an initial downturn related to shifts in U.S. trade policy, the markets quickly recovered as investors determined that tariffs would have only a minimal impact on the world economy. In addition, the backdrop of positive global growth and the increasingly accommodative policies of major central banks proved highly supportive for risk assets. Together, these factors allowed the benchmark to finish June at an all-time high despite the early sell-off. Currency translation provided an additional boost to returns at a time of pronounced weakness in the U.S. dollar. All countries in the index posted gains, with Europe leading the way behind expectations for increased fiscal spending in Germany and interest rate cuts by the European Central Bank.

Contributors and detractors

The fund outperformed in 7 of the 10 sectors in which it held a position, with the best results coming from industrials, energy, and healthcare. Notably, the fund outpaced the broad-based benchmark even though the value style lagged in the quarter.

The British aerospace/defense companies Babcock International Group PLC and BAE Systems PLC, both of which benefited from rising defense spending across Europe, were the largest contributors in industrials. The Italian oil services company Saipem S.p.A was the top contributor in energy. The stock strongly outpaced its sector peers after reporting better-than-expected quarterly earnings and confirming its guidance for the full year. Sandoz Group AG, a Swiss company that focuses on generic pharmaceuticals and biosimilars, was the key driver of outperformance in healthcare. The South Korean internet search and advertisement provider Naver Corp. was the leading contributor in the fund as a whole. The company maintained a dominant market position, and investor sentiment improved on enthusiasm about artificial intelligence-driven enhancements to advertising technology.

Consumer discretionary, information technology, and consumer staples were the only sectors in which the fund underperformed. At the individual stock level, the Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. was the largest detractor. The stock finished with a negative return due to concerns about the slowing Chinese economy, ongoing trade tensions between the United States and China, and cautious investor sentiment toward the Chinese technology sector. Nomad Foods Ltd. (U.K.) and Suzuki Motor Corp. (Japan) were also prominent detractors.

Sector allocation detracted modestly and offset some of the benefit from selection. All of the shortfall was the result of an overweight in energy, which lagged the broader market due to weakness in oil prices. On the other hand, an underweight in consumer discretionary contributed.

Portfolio changes

At the close of the period, the fund had sizable underweights in Europe, Japan, and the Asia-Pacific region, together with overweights in the United Kingdom, North America, and the emerging markets. At the sector level, its largest overweights were in energy, industrials, and materials, while consumer discretionary, information technology, and healthcare were its most notable underweights.

We expect volatility will remain elevated in the coming months. Tariff news in the United States, conflicts overseas, and uncertainty regarding central bank policy could all affect investor sentiment. We welcome volatile market conditions on the belief that they provide fertile ground for us to employ our bottom-up approach to stock selection.
